full_response:
Step 1   Choose a convenient inertial frame  
Take an inertial frame S whose spatial origin is fixed at the wheel’s hub.  
•  In S the hub is always at rest.  
•  Every builder moves on a circle of constant radius r with the common angular velocity ω.  
•  The linear speed of a builder at radius r is  
  v(r)=ωr.  
We are told that the rim (r = R) moves at 0.8 c, so  

  ω = v(R)/R = 0.8c / R.  (1)

Step 2   Proper time accumulated by a builder  
Using cylindrical coordinates (t,r,θ,z) in flat space–time,

 ds² = −c²dt² + dr² + r²dθ² + dz².

For every builder dr = dz = 0 and θ = ωt, so dθ = ωdt.  
Hence along a builder’s world-line

 ds² = −c²dt² + r²ω²dt²  
   = −c²dt² ( 1 − v²/c² ), with v = ωr. (2)

By definition ds = −c dτ, therefore

 dτ = dt √(1 − v²/c²) = dt / γ(r), where γ(r)=1/√(1−v²/c²). (3)

Step 3   A “work day” of length T in the inertial frame  
Let T be the coordinate-time interval between “morning” and “evening” in frame S (this is the meeting condition stated in the problem).  
Integrating (3):

 Δτ(r) = ∫₀ᵗ dt / γ(r) = T / γ(r) (because γ(r) is constant in time). (4)

Step 4   Express γ(r) in terms of the given rim speed  
From (1):

 β(r) ≡ v(r)/c = 0.8 (r/R).  
 γ(r) = 1 / √[1 − β(r)² ] = 1 / √[1 − 0.64 (r/R)² ]. (5)

Insert (5) into (4):

 Δτ(r) = T √[1 − 0.64 (r/R)² ]. (6)

Step 5   Age comparison  
•  Builder at the hub (r = 0): Δτ(0) = T.  
•  Builder at some radius r:  Δτ(r) = T √[1 − 0.64 (r/R)² ].  
•  Builder on the rim (r = R): Δτ(R) = T √(1 − 0.64) = T·0.6.

Therefore all builders are NOT the same age at day’s end: the farther from the hub, the smaller the proper time elapsed, i.e. the less they have aged.  
The age difference between a builder at radius r and one at the hub is

 Δage(0→r) = Δτ(0) − Δτ(r)  
     = T [1 − √(1 − 0.64 (r/R)² ) ]. (7)

For the rim worker the loss of ageing is 40 % of the inertial-frame day.

Assumptions  
1. The hub defines an inertial frame; space-time is flat (no real gravity).  
2. The wheel is “rigid enough” that ω is the same for all radii (classical rigidity is impossible relativistically, but the calculation of elapsed proper time along given world-lines is still valid).  
3. Acceleration per se does not enter; only the speed relative to S matters for time dilation.

\boxed{\displaystyle \Delta\tau(r)=T\sqrt{\,1-\;0.64\left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\,},\qquad
\Delta\text{age}(0\to r)=T\Bigl[1-\sqrt{1-0.64(r/R)^{2}}\Bigr]}  

Hence builders farther from the hub age less; the rim worker ages only 60 % as much as the hub worker during the same workday in the inertial frame.

Confidence: 82%
